posts.name: Nate Simon
posts.cooked:
A reasonably thoughtful peace on the myriad reasons behind the sharp escalations in tasting room fees, at least in Napa.
Labor, insurance, other overhead costs play in to a significant degree.
However, there’s also this: “Other wineries saw that by restricting appointments, and charging a higher fee, they could filter out the casual partyer who just wants to sling back a few drinks. A higher tasting fee self-selects for customers who are likely to buy more bottles of wine at the end of their session — which is, after all, any winery’s main objective.”

San Francisco Chronicle – 22 Jul 22

Napa wine tasting fees are more than twice as expensive as 6 years ago....

An ‘elevated’ wine tasting in Napa now costs $82 per person on average, data shows....

posts.name: Marc Hauser
posts.cooked: So, it’s a profit deal?

posts.name: Al Osterheld
posts.cooked:
Absolutely, crowd control is the best reason to charge a higher tasting fee, and the revenue can be used to provide a better experience. Not really new. Many years ago (early to mid 1980s) very few Napa wineries charged a fee. Sterling Vineyards, which was more high end back then, charged $10 to take the tram up to the tasting room which was the only way to get there, and it was refundable if you bought a bottle. It was one of the places I used to take visitors because it was much more civilized (sit down at tables with a little food) and I always bought bottles. Other wineries quickly started following suit with fees, and sometimes higher fees to get into a separate area with higher end wines. Only makes sense.

-Al

posts.name: Ian Sutton
posts.cooked: Are the tasting fees typically refundable against purchases? I believe that’s been a common approach when there are charges in Australia.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
It is behind a paywall, but can figure from the above the gist of it.
All in favor; there is a cost involved running a tasting room. Resources have been allocated; no reason not to make a profit on them, so long as the price is reasonable enough not to deter customers and potential customers.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
Absolutely, crowd control is the best reason to charge a higher tasting fee, and the revenue can be used to provide a better experience. Not really new. Many years ago (early to mid 1980s) very few Napa wineries charged a fee. …

+1

As I recall, Napa County began requiring tasting fees (in the 90s?) to curb excess drinking, but grandfathered in some wineries.

posts.name: Marc Hauser
posts.cooked:
Are the tasting fees typically refundable against purchases? I believe that’s been a common approach when there are charges in Australia.

Usually, yes.

posts.name: Ian Sutton
posts.cooked:
It is behind a paywall, but can figure from the above the gist of it.
All in favor; there is a cost involved running a tasting room. Resources have been allocated; no reason not to make a profit on them, so long as the price is reasonable enough not to deter customers and potential customers.

Where staff are employed, it absolutely makes sense.

As for profit, there’s certainly a balance equation of tasting room profit vs. ‘curating’ a loyal customer base, but where that equation may well involve a minimal fee for the benefit of loyal customers (to avoid the experience of a tour bus crowd ruining the experience for others).

It can be a very different equation where it’s just family members involved and so their ‘costs’ are already somewhat sunk into the business. For them, they can treat it more as an opportunity to meet and engage with current and future customers, helping to create a stronger bond through that engagement. That’s where many Italian wineries are and the experience on the whole remains a very good one. I’ve never encountered anyone there solely to ‘get some free booze’, but then Italians’ attitude to alcohol is on the whole more respectful / restrained, perhaps as they are often exposed to it in moderation from a young age (“un po’ con acqua”), plus there remains a social stigma about getting wasted by alcohol, whereas in the UK, it’s strangely seen as a funny thing to do

posts.name: Markus S
posts.cooked:
Absolutely, crowd control is the best reason to charge a higher tasting fee, and the revenue can be used to provide a better experience. Not really new. Many years ago (early to mid 1980s) very few Napa wineries charged a fee.

I would even say into the 1990’s. I remember very, very few charging in 1993.

posts.name: Nate Simon
posts.cooked:
So…charge more money to keep customers OUT? Honestly, this makes little sense to me. In the short term, I’m sure it works for that purpose. However, in the long term, you are excluding people who may want to “get in on the ground level“ and just try some wines and learn.
I began learning about wine in college. As some have mentioned, you could go up to Napa and visit and taste, and the fees were very modest.
Today, how would that even work? That college student or young professional, who one day may be an affluent person with the disposable income to buy quantities of wine? He or she may not end up as a customer, since they were not able to explore wine earlier in life. Beer and spirits might be their preference, and those palatial Napa wineries, with their sky-high operating costs, will wonder where everyone went. Just a thought.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
So…charge more money to keep customers OUT? Honestly, this makes little sense to me. In the short term, I’m sure it works for that purpose. However, in the long term, you are excluding people who may want to “get in on the ground level“ and just try some wines and learn.
I began learning about wine in college. As some have mentioned, you could go up to Napa and visit and taste, and the fees were very modest.
Today, how would that even work? That college student or young professional, who one day may be an affluent person with the disposable income to buy quantities of wine? He or she may not end up as a customer, since they were not able to explore wine earlier in life. Beer and spirits might be their preference, and those palatial Napa wineries, with their sky-high operating costs, will wonder where everyone went. Just a thought.

If you’ve ever visited Napa tasting rooms on a summer weekend, and seen the gangs of tipsy tasters elbowing their way to the counter, it’s easy to see why the wineries see little value in freebies. Better to force people to make some decisions about how many wineries to visit and which they are actually interested in.

posts.name: Jeff M 0 L l
posts.cooked: With some exceptions, I am typically visiting wineries I already purchase from and get the tasting comped because of that. I like being able to taste what I am buying without opening my own bottles and maybe I find something else they make that I want to buy as well. On the flip side of that, if you want to get to know a winery a tour and tasting is a great opportunity to see what they have to offer.

posts.name: Scott Brunson
posts.cooked:
Maybe these wineries are trying to maximize profits before the bubble bursts.

Seriously, I have no problems with fees increasing as long as the fee, or at least a large portion of it, can be applied to a purchase.
One of my most frustrating winery visits occurred when the winery waived the fee with purchase, but then only offered lesser wines since the wines I liked were “allocated.” So I paid our fees and told the employee that I would just spend all of my budget at a different winery.
And I did.

^this. Plus, something that I haven’t seen noted yet is the disastrous effect a drunk driver could possibly have on a winery’s license. In Oregon, a winery or bar can be held liable if a drunk driver causes damage. I don’t know how often that exactly happens, but it is enough of a possibility that it is strongly considered. I don’t have a server’s license for California, but I wouldn’t be all too surprised if there is/are similar law(s) on the books and that alone is more than enough of a deterrent to go to a model of taking appointments and charging more for them.

posts.name: larry schaffer

Who decides what a ‘reasonable’ fee is? Nate’s point is a good one - can’t there be something ‘reasonable’ in between ‘free’ and ‘exorbitant’??

Cheers.

posts.name: Ąņɗŗęŵ Ķļϋǥ
posts.cooked:
So…charge more money to keep customers OUT? Honestly, this makes little sense to me.

I think you have not run a business before. Can you really call yourself a customer if you just take free samples and leave? Is that the type of “customer” the business wants to attract?

You have limited number of seats in your establishment, the goal is to maximize revenue from each seat. Naturally with something like a winery, revenue is not as simple as a one time transaction. Certainly goodwill that translates into sales later is of value. But there’s ways to evaluate lifetime value of different types of customers and curate your offerings to bring more of those customers and fewer of the less desirable customers.

Exactly. I was told years ago that some wineries in Napa were pouring a very significant percentage of their overall production in the tasting room and not generating an amount of sales that made sense. When that becomes the reality, something has to change. SO many people visit Napa for the experience, with no intention of buying wine. I’ve even seen a bit of that in Oregon. And those are the loud, obnoxious parties making the experience much worse for the people who are there to buy wine. Evaluating wine with a ton of loud noise in the background (or right next to you) is just about impossible.

As far as going to a wine region to learn, it works in places that haven’t become popular tourist destinations.

posts.name: John Flanagan
posts.cooked:
I like that the tasting room fees can result in a better experience for those of us that are there to make buying decisions and not just to get drunk. However, as mentioned above, Napa is now a “well-heeled resort area” and the tasting fees reflect that. Not sure what the “minimal fee” is for each winery to keep the buses away, but $75, $100, and even $150 tasting fees per person are not uncommon anymore.

I’ve been going to Napa for over 20 years now, and the sky rocketing (rocketed?) tasting fees have kept me away in recent years, and they have kept me from recommending Napa to newbies. To Nate’s point above, it was the busy — but cheap — tasting rooms that helped me explore wine when I lived in Northern California as a college student in the late ‘90s. There is no way I would have been able to explore so much great wine at that time with the fees the way they are now.

posts.name: Keith A k e r s
posts.cooked:
Who decides what a ‘reasonable’ fee is? Nate’s point is a good one - can’t there be something ‘reasonable’ in between ‘free’ and ‘exorbitant’??

Cheers.

Reasonable is in the eyes of the beholder. When I visited Cade and Anderson Conn in the before days, I was more than happy to pay close to $100 even being itb. Great views, smaller crowd (or just me) and a more personal experience. Dealing with drunks if it was an open tasting with no reservations and a lower price would’ve taken away greatly from it.

Do you enjoy it when people come to you, get shot faced on the cheap and buy absolutely nothing? What’s reasonable for you also likely isn’t reasonable in Napa and you aren’t aiming at the same clientele.

posts.name: Doug Schulman
posts.cooked:
Who decides what a ‘reasonable’ fee is? Nate’s point is a good one - can’t there be something ‘reasonable’ in between ‘free’ and ‘exorbitant’??

Cheers.

I guess one would try to set a price that kept the tasting room profitable to a reasonable degree. I think reasonable for the winery has to define reasonable for the customer. If the winery charges way too much, people won’t come. Way too little, the winery could be losing money on the tasting room (and some have). Some people’s opinions shouldn’t trump basic, pragmatic business decisions, IMO.

posts.name: Nate Simon
posts.cooked:
Who decides what a ‘reasonable’ fee is? Nate’s point is a good one - can’t there be something ‘reasonable’ in between ‘free’ and ‘exorbitant’??

Who decides what an exorbitant fee is?

The answer to all of this is ‘what the market will bear’.

The consumer decides, of course.
I’ve decided that most Napa tasting fees are exorbitant.
I still go to Napa (an easy day trip for me), but not very often, and almost always with prior appointments.
That being said, those gangs of tipsy tasters have credit cards that are as valid as anyone else’s.
The idea (stated in the article) that charging more for tasting will select for visitors who are more likely to buy bottles makes sense superficially, but is illogical at some level. Charge more so people will buy more stuff? There must be a logical/economic end to this line of reasoning. The wines get more expensive, and tasting them gets more expensive, and wineries will price themselves out on both counts.

It makes sense on every level, particularly if some or all of the price of the tasting can be refunded on purchase. It incentivizes the customer to buy. Besides, I imagine in these days of analytics that wineries have figured out the visitor to buyer ratio, and the best use of the tasting room.

posts.name: Nate Simon

Charge more so people will buy more stuff? There must be a logical/economic end to this line of reasoning. The wines get more expensive, and tasting them gets more expensive, and wineries will price themselves out on both counts.

It makes sense on every level, particularly if some or all of the price of the tasting can be refunded on purchase. It incentivizes the customer to buy. Besides, I imagine in these days of analytics that wineries have figured out the visitor to buyer ratio, and the best use of the tasting room.

It makes sense, perhaps, for some portion of the buying public, at some times.
If I plan to buy, or have a strong likelihood of buying, I have no problem going (assuming the tasting fee is credited to purchase, which apparently isn’t as universal as I assumed).
However, I don’t always want to feel obligated to buy, and sometimes simply want to have a nice visit and try some new wines.
This is a bit of a thread drift, but I wonder, for the average winery, how big a revenue center their tasting room actually is. I’m sure this varies widely based on many factors. I often wonder if wineries see their tasting rooms as opportunities to substantially improve sales and consumer exposure, or more as an obligatory aspect of the business that costs more than it brings in.

posts.name: Markus S
posts.cooked:

So how much would you have charged a young person - not professional salaried - staying at an inexpensive motel ($50) back in the day? To them they were clearly trying to learn, not impress or be impressed with anything except the wine and the people.

No, it’s a time deal. Though as the saying goes, “time is money”. So, I guess you are correct.


That said, I achieve the same goals by not charging a fee at all, and only promoting my wines here…

It makes sense, perhaps, for some portion of the buying public, at some times.
If I plan to buy, or have a strong likelihood of buying, I have no problem going (assuming the tasting fee is credited to purchase, which apparently isn’t as universal as I assumed).
However, I don’t always want to feel obligated to buy, and sometimes simply want to have a nice visit and try some new wines.
This is a bit of a thread drift, but I wonder, for the average winery, how big a revenue center their tasting room actually is. I’m sure this varies widely based on many factors. I often wonder if wineries see their tasting rooms as opportunities to substantially improve sales and consumer exposure, or more as an obligatory aspect of the business that costs more than it brings in.

You post a good question. My honest answer is that it probably depends upon how successfully the winery has established their tasting room.

We have some very successful tasting rooms in the area, notably Domaine Drouhin, Sokol Blosser, Stoller, and Soter. These are bigger TRs and they are well staffed, very accommodating, and typically very friendly visits. They also probably have substantial overheads to manage, so I could only guess at profitability. But all of these wineries have relatively wide distribution, and their hospitality goes a long way to helping create orders from guests when they return home as well. So even if they see quite a few casual vacationers/tire kickers, it’s probably well worth it.

There are also TRs in the area that push to be like these wineries and probably do reasonable business but normally wind up with less revenue for effort than they would like to see. I say normally because there is a real upswing over the past 2 years in visitors to the Valley and as far as I can tell, just about all winery DTC sales are up. But prior to that competition made it much tougher. But even with the upswing, many wineries are retaining the appointment only status that evolved as TRs were allowed to open again.


For me though, the single most valuable aspect of winery visits is not the revenue (it is very helpful though) but the opportunity to speak about terroir in the Willamette Valley and what our goals are for the wines directly to wine enthusiasts and collectors. When our wines go out to other states through distribution, the entry level wines make up 90% of what distributors look for. People visiting want to see the vineyard designates and micro-lots, which are the wines that I want to make.

Honestly, I feel that many wineries overspend on TRs and wind up with higher fixed costs and extra labor costs to manage their TRs. I spent 17 years in restaurants and have never considered managing a tasting room. But it’s easy enough to have a table and just pour for people when they come visit. Our winery is as non-fancy as you can possibly imagine, but that really seems to be something that people visiting really enjoy. I have zero overhead, except the time Megan and I spend and the bottles we open. Both are significant costs, but are more than covered by the purchases and generosity of everyone who visits. Carter Pelham just came through with his wife and friends and brought Megan and I a bottle of Cedric Bouchard as a gift, which is unexpected but very appreciated. Over the years we’ve received a lot of great bottles from Berserkers, and having a more personalized relationship with visitors is more meaningful to me and also good business.

posts.name: Marcus Goodfellow
posts.cooked:
Who decides what an exorbitant fee is?

The answer to all of this is ‘what the market will bear’.

The consumer decides, of course.
I’ve decided that most Napa tasting fees are exorbitant.
I still go to Napa (an easy day trip for me), but not very often, and almost always with prior appointments.
That being said, those gangs of tipsy tasters have credit cards that are as valid as anyone else’s.
The idea (stated in the article) that charging more for tasting will select for visitors who are more likely to buy bottles makes sense superficially, but is illogical at some level. Charge more so people will buy more stuff? There must be a logical/economic end to this line of reasoning. The wines get more expensive, and tasting them gets more expensive, and wineries will price themselves out on both counts.

It makes sense on every level, particularly if some or all of the price of the tasting can be refunded on purchase. It incentivizes the customer to buy. Besides, I imagine in these days of analytics that wineries have figured out the visitor to buyer ratio, and the best use of the tasting room.

In a competitive market, competition will keep pricing in check. When demand exceeds supply, pricing goes up.

As wine lovers, we hate it. I am very frustrated with the pricing on Clos Rougeard now, but it’s still just the market at work.

And to be fair, the gangs of tipsy tasters may have credit cards, but are they using them in a meaningful way? If I was in Burgundy and could taste at DRC for $50, I would do it in a heartbeat as it would be a chance to taste DRC, but I would still do my buying in MSD, Chambolle, Volnay, and Pernand. Is it fair for DRC to pour for me, if there’s no way that I going to be able to buy? Maybe, lots of people will increase their financial status and pouring for them could be an investment in the future (this is our outlook, though more from a tannin/acid standpoint as our pricing is pretty reasonable), but I also understand why a winery would opt not to.

posts.name: Ph!l M@rx
posts.cooked: I view my infrequent trips to Napa (and other wine regions) as my equivalent to going to Disney World - it may be pricey, but it is worth it for the memorable experiences. There has also been an upward progression in what is provided during “by appointment-only” tastings, from the food to the glassware to the amount of time spent with staff. I have no problem with wineries charging what the market will bear and frankly, it is none of my business if they are doing to cut down on traffic, cover costs of providing the tasting or earning a profit. I also completely appreciate that others may feel differently and/or long for the “good old days”.

A higher tasting fee self-selects for customers who are likely to buy more bottles of wine at the end of their session — which is, after all, any winery’s main objective.”
https://www.sfchronicle.com/food/wine/article/Napa-wine-tasting-17320491.php

As you pointed out, it’s one of a myriad of reasons, not the main one, but if you had to deal with bachelorette parties taking over the tasting room, a high fee helps. Of course there isn’t as many face plants in the parking lot either.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked: As a footnote, the last time I was in Piedmont, some wineries had begun charging for tastings. I’d never encountered that on six or seven previous visits. But tourism there has increased many fold over the last 25 years.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
So…charge more money to keep customers OUT? Honestly, this makes little sense to me. In the short term, I’m sure it works for that purpose. However, in the long term, you are excluding people who may want to “get in on the ground level“ and just try some wines and learn.
I began learning about wine in college. As some have mentioned, you could go up to Napa and visit and taste, and the fees were very modest.
Today, how would that even work? That college student or young professional, who one day may be an affluent person with the disposable income to buy quantities of wine? He or she may not end up as a customer, since they were not able to explore wine earlier in life. Beer and spirits might be their preference, and those palatial Napa wineries, with their sky-high operating costs, will wonder where everyone went. Just a thought.

If you’ve ever visited Napa tasting rooms on a summer weekend, and seen the gangs of tipsy tasters elbowing their way to the counter, it’s easy to see why the wineries see little value in freebies. Better to force people to make some decisions about how many wineries to visit and which they are actually interested in.

When I lived in the valley, I was amused to learn that local uber drivers referred to a certain winery in the county as “Del Blotto”…

posts.name: Robert M yers
posts.cooked:
I have to admit I always wanted to do one of those cave tours with David Del Dotto as they sounded legendary. (And were not cheap) I always heard David Arthur was a blast as well.

Unless it’s a personal tour with the winemaker though, I’d just assume walk in and encounter one of those machines where you can buy a pour by the ounce. And have a person or two available to answer questions. I’ve been to too many where the interactions from your assigned person is cringeworthy.

Just think when the bachelorette party rolls in they could dial the price per ounce up so fast and bank

fancy_title: Mobley on the escalating prices in tasting rooms
title: Mobley on the escalating prices in tasting rooms
